# Predicting Body Segment Properties


<!-- WARNING: THIS FILE WAS AUTOGENERATED! DO NOT EDIT! -->

*A tool for predicting body segment properties based on the data set ANSURII*
<!--This file will become your README and also the index of your
documentation.-->

## About

<!--En kort sammanfattning av vad projektet gör och varför det finns.-->

This tool predicts body segment properties — including **mass**, **center of mass**, and **moment of inertia** — from anthropometric measurements based on the **ANSURII** dataset (U.S. Army Anthropometric Survey).

The goal is to enable easier estimation of biomechanical properties for use in simulations, ergonomics, or health-related analyses.


<!--This is a tool made for predicting body segement properties of a human body. The the tool predicts measurements of a human body based on the dataset ANSURII. From the predicted measurements mass, center of mass and moment of inertia are predicted.-->

### Supported File Formats (fixa kommentarerna)

- **Input:** `.csv` — containing raw anthropometric measurements.
- **Output:** `.csv` (predicted values), `.png` (optional plots), others coming soon.


### Original Creators
Augusta Fhager, Parsa Khodabakhsh, Sanna Lingsten, Theodor Roegner Kinnmark, Elinor Schwartz


## Instalation 



## Developer Guide

If you are new to using `nbdev` here are some useful pointers to get you
started.

### Install anthropmass in Development mode

``` sh
# make sure anthropmass package is installed in development mode
$ pip install -e .

# make changes under nbs/ directory
# ...

# compile to have changes apply to anthropmass
$ nbdev_prepare
```

## Usage

### Installation

Install latest from the GitHub
[repository](https://github.com/sannasann/anthropmass):

``` sh
$ pip install git+https://github.com/sannasann/anthropmass.git
```

or from [conda](https://anaconda.org/sannasann/anthropmass)

``` sh
$ conda install -c sannasann anthropmass
```

or from [pypi](https://pypi.org/project/anthropmass/)

``` sh
$ pip install anthropmass
```

### Documentation

Documentation can be found hosted on this GitHub
[repository](https://github.com/sannasann/anthropmass)’s
[pages](https://sannasann.github.io/anthropmass/). Additionally you can
find package manager specific guidelines on
[conda](https://anaconda.org/sannasann/anthropmass) and
[pypi](https://pypi.org/project/anthropmass/) respectively.

# Predicting Body Segment Properties

*A tool for predicting body segment properties based on the ANSUR II dataset.*

<!-- WARNING: THIS FILE WAS AUTOGENERATED! DO NOT EDIT! -->

<!-- This file will become your README and also the index of your documentation. -->

---

## About

This tool predicts body segment properties, including **mass**, **center of mass**, and **moment of inertia**, from anthropometric measurements based on the **ANSUR II** dataset (U.S. Army Anthropometric Survey).

The goal is to enable easier estimation of biomechanical properties for use in simulations, ergonomics, or health-related analyses.

---

## Supported File Formats

* **Input:** `.csv` — containing raw anthropometric measurements.
* **Output:** `.csv` (predicted values), `.png` (optional plots), others coming soon.

---

## Original Creators

Augusta Fhager, Parsa Khodabakhsh, Sanna Lingsten, Theodor Roegner Kinnmark, Elinor Schwartz

---

##  Installation

Install via `pip`:

```bash
$ pip install anthropmass
```

Install latest development version from GitHub:

```bash
$ pip install git+https://github.com/sannasann/anthropmass.git
```

Or from conda:

```bash
$ conda install -c sannasann anthropmass
```

---

## Developer Guide

If you're using `nbdev` to develop:

```bash
# Install package in development mode
$ pip install -e .

# Make changes under nbs/ and compile
$ nbdev_prepare
```

---

## Documentation

Complete documentation is available at: [https://sannasann.github.io/anthropmass](https://sannasann.github.io/anthropmass)

Also available on:

* [PyPI](https://pypi.org/project/anthropmass/)
* [Conda](https://anaconda.org/sannasann/anthropmass)

---

##  Usage Example

Here’s a minimal usage example:

```python
from anthropmass import predictor

# Load your input CSV (anthropometric data)
result = predictor("input_measurements.csv")

# Save output
result.to_csv("predicted_segments.csv")
```
